name: UK
title: UK Political Quiz 2026 | CoreOpinion
desc: Take the free UK political compass quiz. 47 scenario questions to find where you stand and check your alignment with British political parties.
keywords: UK political quiz, British political compass test, Labour Conservative quiz 2026, Reform UK test, where do I stand in British politics, political alignment test UK, which party should I vote for UK, party quiz Britain

lab.name: Labour
lab.desc: Your answers most closely match the Labour Party. You tend to support public services, workers rights, and government intervention to reduce inequality.

con.name: Conservative
con.desc: Your answers most closely match the Conservative Party. You tend to favour lower taxes, a smaller state, and traditional institutions.

lib.name: Liberal Democrats
lib.desc: Your answers most closely match the Liberal Democrats. You favour individual freedom, strong public services, and closer ties with Europe.

ref.name: Reform UK
ref.desc: Your answers most closely match Reform UK. You want radical cuts to immigration, NHS reform, and a rejection of the mainstream political consensus.

grn.name: Green Party
grn.desc: Your answers most closely match the Green Party. You prioritise environmental action, wealth redistribution, and systemic economic change.

snp.name: SNP
snp.desc: Your answers most closely match the SNP. You support Scottish independence, centre-left economics, and a closer relationship with Europe.

bank.ctx: Record waiting lists. 7.5 million people waiting for treatment. A and E corridors full. GPs impossible to get.
bank.q: The NHS is collapsing under the weight of underfunding. It needs a massive injection of public money now.

opts.t: Yes. Tax and invest. The NHS is a national treasure and it is dying.
More money yes, but structural reform is equally necessary.
The problem is inefficiency, not funding. Reform before more cash.
The NHS model is broken. Private insurance and competition would serve patients better.

bank.q: The NHS wastes billions on management, consultants, and bureaucracy. No amount of new money will fix it without root-and-branch reform.

opts.t: Yes. The management layer is bloated and must be cut.
There is waste, but the same argument is used to justify any cut.
No. The NHS needs more staff, not fewer managers.

bank.q: People who can afford private healthcare should be encouraged to use it, freeing up NHS capacity for those who cannot.

opts.t: Yes. A mixed system serves everyone better.
As a temporary measure while the NHS recovers, perhaps. Not as a long-term model.
No. Two-tier healthcare is a slippery slope to dismantling the NHS entirely.

bank.ctx: Net migration hit 900,000 in 2023. Housing costs are soaring. Schools are stretched. Public services are under pressure.
bank.q: Mass immigration has been allowed to run out of control and ordinary people are paying the price.

opts.t: Yes. Numbers must come down sharply and quickly.
That number is misleading. Most migrants are NHS nurses and care workers we desperately need.
Immigration is not the problem. Chronic underinvestment in public services is.

bank.ctx: The NHS employs over 100,000 workers from India and other countries. Social care would collapse without overseas staff.
bank.q: Britain depends on immigration to function. Cutting it would cause serious harm.

opts.t: Yes. We should be grateful, not hostile.
We should train our own people rather than importing labour indefinitely.
Managed migration is fine. Uncontrolled mass migration is different.

bank.q: Asylum seekers who arrive in the UK by small boat across the Channel should be removed to a safe third country.

opts.t: Yes. Illegal entry must have consequences or the system collapses.
The Rwanda scheme was cruel and unworkable. Legal safe routes are the answer.
People seeking asylum are exercising a legal right. Criminalising them is wrong.

bank.q: Britain has a legal and moral obligation to accept refugees from war and persecution, regardless of how they arrived.

opts.t: Yes. The mode of arrival does not cancel the obligation.
We have obligations, but they are not unlimited and must be managed.
No. International obligations must be balanced against national capacity.

bank.ctx: Energy bills have doubled. Grocery prices are up 25% since 2021. Food bank use has tripled. Real wages are below 2008 levels.
bank.q: This is not bad luck. It is the result of political choices and corporate greed.

opts.t: Yes. Windfall taxes, price caps, and a proper living wage.
Some intervention is right but markets generally correct themselves.
Intervention makes it worse. Cut taxes and regulation so businesses can cut prices.

bank.q: Raising the minimum wage too fast kills jobs for the young and low-skilled people it is meant to help.

opts.t: Yes. Businesses cannot absorb rapid increases and workers suffer.
The evidence from the past decade shows this fear is overblown.
No. Workers kept on poverty wages subsidise corporate profits.

bank.ctx: Average house price: 9 times average salary in England. 1.2 million people on social housing waiting lists. Generation Rent cannot buy.
bank.q: The housing crisis is the defining injustice of our time and requires emergency government action.

opts.t: Yes. Build hundreds of thousands of social homes and end Right to Buy.
Reform planning to allow private housebuilding. Let the market solve it.
Both. Reform planning AND build social housing simultaneously.

bank.q: Landlords provide a vital service. Rent controls and punitive taxes will drive them out and make the housing crisis worse.

opts.t: Yes. Landlords respond to incentives and driving them out shrinks supply.
Good landlords are fine. The sector needs regulation, not abolition.
No. Private landlordism at this scale is extracting wealth from renters. It needs curbing.

bank.ctx: The UK has the highest tax burden in 70 years. Public services are still deteriorating.
bank.q: We are taxed more than ever and getting less than ever. Something is fundamentally broken.

opts.t: Yes. The money is being wasted. Cut tax and cut the state.
Tax is high because of 14 years of underinvestment creating crises that cost more.
Tax rises were necessary. Spending reform is needed alongside them.

bank.q: Britain has some of the highest inheritance taxes in the world. Taxing wealth people have already paid tax on once is double punishment.

opts.t: Yes. Inheritance tax hits ordinary families and should be scrapped or dramatically cut.
The threshold should protect family homes but very large estates should pay their share.
No. Inherited wealth entrenches inequality. Inheritance tax is the right response.

bank.q: The wealthy do not pay enough tax. A proper wealth tax on assets above 10 million pounds would raise billions without harming most people.

opts.t: Yes. Extreme wealth should be taxed to fund public services.
A wealth tax is administratively difficult and the rich will find ways around it.
No. Wealth taxes destroy investment and drive capital offshore.

bank.ctx: The UK committed to net zero by 2050. The previous government delayed several climate targets. Extreme weather events are increasing.
bank.q: Net zero is not a political choice. It is a survival necessity and Britain must lead.

opts.t: Yes. Accelerate the transition. Green investment creates jobs and energy security.
The target is right but the transition must not destroy working-class livelihoods.
Net zero by 2050 is unaffordable and will cost ordinary families dearly.

bank.q: Blocking new North Sea oil and gas licences while importing fossil fuels from abroad is both economically illiterate and environmentally pointless.

opts.t: Yes. Domestic production makes sense while we transition.
The jobs argument has merit but long-term we must stop extracting fossil fuels.
No. New oil fields lock in decades of emissions. The argument for them is a fossil fuel lobby talking point.

bank.ctx: Private school pupils are 7 times more likely to attend Oxbridge than state school pupils. Private schools receive 1.8 billion in VAT exemptions.
bank.q: Private schools buy advantage at public expense. Their tax breaks should end.

opts.t: Yes. Remove all charitable status and VAT exemptions for private schools.
Remove the tax breaks but do not seek to abolish private education itself.
No. Parents who pay twice, through taxes and fees, deserve recognition not punishment.

bank.q: University has been oversold. Too many young people leave with debts and degrees that do not lead to jobs. Apprenticeships and vocational routes deserve equal esteem.

opts.t: Yes. We have pushed too many people into universities that are not right for them.
Both paths matter. The problem is the debt, not the education.
University should be free. The argument against it is a class argument dressed as economics.

bank.ctx: The UK has some of the weakest workers rights in Europe. Zero-hours contracts, fire and rehire, minimum wage enforcement all rank poorly.
bank.q: British workers have been stripped of protections that Europeans take for granted. It is time to restore them.

opts.t: Yes. Day one rights, banning zero-hours contracts, proper sick pay.
Some reforms are overdue but the flexibility of the UK labour market is also a strength.
Flexible labour markets attract investment. Over-regulation destroys jobs.

bank.q: Trade unions have too much power and Labour is in their pocket. Strike action costs the economy billions and holds the public hostage.

opts.t: Yes. Unions protect their own at the expense of everyone else.
The right to strike is fundamental. But some essential services need better protection.
No. Workers striking for decent pay and conditions is democracy in action.

bank.q: Knife crime is out of control in British cities. Softer sentencing and police cuts have consequences.

opts.t: Yes. Longer sentences, more police, stop and search.
The causes are poverty and cuts to youth services. Address those.
Both deterrence and prevention are needed. One without the other fails.

bank.q: Stop and search disproportionately targets young Black men and damages community trust in the police. It must be constrained.

opts.t: Yes. The evidence of racial disparity is clear and reforms are needed.
With proper oversight it can be effective. Abolishing it entirely would be wrong.
No. Stop and search saves lives and its restriction is ideological, not evidential.

bank.q: First past the post is a relic that lets governments win landslide majorities on a third of the vote. Britain needs proportional representation.

opts.t: Yes. PR would produce fairer results and more genuine coalition-building.
FPTP produces strong governments. PR produces weak coalitions and instability.
The system needs reform, but PR alone does not solve the problem of bad politics.

bank.q: The House of Lords is an embarrassment. Unelected peers with no mandate making law is indefensible in a modern democracy.

opts.t: Yes. Abolish it or replace it with an elected chamber.
Remove hereditary peers and make it smaller, but keep expertise through appointments.
Appointed expertise provides a useful check on elected politicians. Keep it roughly as is.

bank.q: Scotland should have the right to hold a second independence referendum if its parliament votes for one.

opts.t: Yes. It is a fundamental democratic right.
Only with agreement between both governments and with clear criteria for what constitutes a settled question.
No. The 2014 vote settled this for a generation. The constitution cannot be relitigated every decade.

bank.q: Devolution has gone too far. England subsidises Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland while English voters have less democratic power than any other part of the UK.

opts.t: Yes. England needs its own parliament and the Barnett formula must go.
The funding formula needs updating but full English devolution risks breaking the Union.
No. Devolution strengthens the UK by giving nations ownership of their own affairs.

bank.ctx: UK defence spending is 2.3% of GDP. The government has pledged to raise it to 2.5% by 2027 and then 3%.
bank.q: In a more dangerous world, spending more on defence is not a luxury. It is a necessity.

opts.t: Yes. NATO commitments and Russian aggression require serious investment.
Spend smarter, not just more. Reform how the military operates first.
No. 3% of GDP on defence means 3% not spent on schools, hospitals, and homes.

bank.q: Britain should use its veto at the UN Security Council more aggressively to protect national interests, even if that means blocking resolutions others support.

opts.t: Yes. Britain must be willing to act in its own interests.
The veto is a last resort. Cooperation through multilateral institutions is usually better.
The UN Security Council is outdated. Britain should invest in reforming it.

bank.q: Brexit was the right decision. Britain is better off making its own laws, controlling its own borders, and signing its own trade deals.

opts.t: Yes. Whatever the short-term disruption, sovereignty was worth it.
Brexit was a mistake. Britain should swallow its pride and negotiate the closest possible relationship with the EU.
No. Brexit has damaged the economy, weakened security ties, and isolated Britain.

bank.q: Britain should rejoin the EU single market, even if that means accepting freedom of movement again.

opts.t: Yes. The economic benefits outweigh the political costs.
A closer trading relationship yes, but full single market membership goes too far for now.
No. We voted to control our own borders. That must not be reversed.

bank.ctx: The two-child benefit cap means families with three or more children receive no additional support. 250,000 children are in poverty as a direct result.
bank.q: Punishing children for being born into large families is not welfare reform. It is cruelty.

opts.t: Yes. Scrap the two-child cap immediately.
Limiting child benefit at some point is defensible, even if two children is too few.
Unlimited child benefit creates perverse incentives. The cap should stay.

bank.q: The welfare bill is unsustainable. Too many working-age people are on long-term sickness benefits and the system is being gamed.

opts.t: Yes. The system needs tightening and fraud and abuse must be tackled.
Most people on sickness benefits are genuinely unable to work. Punishing them is wrong.
Reform is needed but the problem is lack of NHS treatment and support, not fraud.

bank.ctx: The UK has among the highest domestic energy prices in Europe. Energy-intensive industries are relocating abroad.
bank.q: Green levies and net zero targets are hollowing out British industry and exporting jobs and emissions.

opts.t: Yes. Pause the green agenda until energy prices come down.
Energy transition costs are real but the answer is industrial strategy, not retreat.
No. The long-term cost of not acting on climate dwarfs any short-term industrial pain.

bank.ctx: The UK has some of the highest drug-related death rates in Europe. Scotland has the worst rate in the developed world.
bank.q: Drug laws have failed. Decriminalisation and a public health approach is the only honest response.

opts.t: Yes. Portugal shows this works. Treat addiction as a health issue, not a crime.
The evidence for decriminalisation is compelling but full legalisation goes too far.
No. Decriminalisation sends the wrong message and increases use.

bank.q: Britain has lost confidence in its own history and culture. We should teach our children to be proud of their country, not ashamed of it.

opts.t: Yes. Patriotism and teaching national history positively is not racism.
Pride in country and honest history are not opposites. We can have both.
No. An honest reckoning with empire and history is what real national confidence looks like.

bank.q: The state is too big, too expensive, and too involved in British life. Cutting it back would make people more free and more prosperous.

opts.t: Yes. A leaner state, lower taxes, and more individual responsibility.
The state does too much in some areas and too little in others. Smarter, not smaller.
No. The state builds the infrastructure, education, and healthcare that makes prosperity possible.

bank.q: The gap between the richest and poorest in Britain is a moral scandal that demands urgent government action.

opts.t: Yes. Wealth taxes, higher top rates, and stronger public services.
Inequality matters, but growth and opportunity are better solutions than redistribution alone.
No. Inequality reflects different efforts and outcomes. Forced equality stifles ambition.

heroTitle: Where do you stand in British politics?
heroSub: 47 scenario-based questions on the issues defining UK politics right now. See your position on the compass and your alignment with every major party.
partyStripHtml:
Labour
Your answers most closely match the Labour Party. You tend to support public services, workers rights, and government intervention to reduce inequality.
Conservative
Your answers most closely match the Conservative Party. You tend to favour lower taxes, a smaller state, and traditional institutions.
Liberal Democrats
Your answers most closely match the Liberal Democrats. You favour individual freedom, strong public services, and closer ties with Europe.
Reform UK
Your answers most closely match Reform UK. You want radical cuts to immigration, NHS reform, and a rejection of the mainstream political consensus.
Green Party
Your answers most closely match the Green Party. You prioritise environmental action, wealth redistribution, and systemic economic change.
